museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: January 2000: The millennium opens with Dreamcast still vivid and PS2 still mostly a rumour for UK buyers.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for January 2000. Regional release dates are deliberately conservative and should be checked before verification.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: The millennium opens with Dreamcast still vivid and PS2 still mostly a rumour for UK buyers.
items.body: A period-context note for the month, written cautiously where the evidence is broader than one exact day.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: PC players watch The Sims and Diablo II previews turn domestic rooms and loot into future obsessions.
items.body: A period-context note for the month, written cautiously where the evidence is broader than one exact day.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: Import columns and Japanese launch coverage make March feel close before it arrives.
items.body: A source-noted month marker, with regional timing kept visible where it matters.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: PS2 anticipation changes how Dreamcast, N64 and PlayStation shelves are read.
items.body: A year-level context marker included to frame the month without claiming a new event happened on a specific day.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: PC gaming feels unusually strong through sims, RPGs, mods and online play.
items.body: A year-level context marker included to frame the month without claiming a new event happened on a specific day.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: The Sims
items.body: Maxis turns the domestic routine into one of PC gaming's strangest mass obsessions.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed as a February 2000 PC release; verify regional retail timing during human edit.

items.title: Resident Evil Code: Veronica
items.body: Capcom gives Dreamcast one of its showcase survival-horror objects.
items.sourceNote: Early 2000 Dreamcast timing is source-noted; exact UK shelf date needs verification.

items.title: PlayStation 2 launch line-up
items.body: In Japan, the console itself is the exhibit: DVD playback, black plastic and next-generation gravity.
items.sourceNote: PlayStation sources list March 4, 2000 for Japan.

items.title: Perfect Dark
items.body: Rare's spy shooter arrives late in the N64 life, dense with modes and memory-expansion ambition.
items.sourceNote: 2000 release lists place Perfect Dark in May; regional dates should be checked.

items.title: Deus Ex
items.body: Ion Storm makes the PC feel conspiratorial, systemic and full of locked doors with multiple answers.
items.sourceNote: GDC narrative review and other references list late June 2000.

items.title: Diablo II
items.body: Blizzard turns clicking, loot and Battle.net into a long-term habit.
items.sourceNote: Commonly listed for late June 2000; use month-level timing unless a final source is selected.

items.title: Tony Hawk's Pro Skater 2
items.body: Skate culture, licensed music and score-chasing become a shared language.
items.sourceNote: Release timing varies by platform and region; September 2000 is safe for the original wave.

items.title: Baldur's Gate II: Shadows of Amn
items.body: BioWare makes the Infinity Engine feel vast, literate and intimidatingly generous.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot reports September 2000 shipping plans.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: PlayStation 2 reaches Europe
items.body: Sony's black console arrives with DVD playback, backwards compatibility and a sense of cultural inevitability.
items.sourceNote: PlayStation sources list the European launch as November 24, 2000.

items.title: Dreamcast fights on
items.body: Sega's machine remains beloved for arcade conversions, online experiments and a library with personality.
items.sourceNote: Dreamcast context is drawn from period coverage and later platform histories.

items.title: N64 enters its late period
items.body: Perfect Dark and Majora's Mask show how much was still possible on aging cartridge hardware.
items.sourceNote: Release calendars place both as late-generation N64 highlights.

items.title: PC gaming looks wonderfully untidy
items.body: The year's PC shelf mixes The Sims, Deus Ex, Diablo II, Baldur's Gate II, Counter-Strike and hardware upgrade anxiety.
items.sourceNote: PC release lists and magazine context support this year-level reading.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Dial-up culture
items.body: Online life still sounds like dial-up tones, message boards, IRC, GameFAQs, fan sites and magazine URLs typed carefully from print.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Servers and services
items.body: Counter-Strike makes the mod scene feel less like a side corridor and more like the main entrance for PC shooters.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Forums and files
items.body: Battle.net turns Diablo II into a nightly ritual of characters, loot and fragile connections.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

items.title: Console online experiments
items.body: Dreamcast online remains a glimpse of a future that most UK living rooms are not quite ready to live in.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period release context and UK access patterns; verify with period magazine/web sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like standing in two shops at once: one full of Dreamcast games, N64 cartridges and PC boxes, the other glowing with a PS2 you might not be able to buy.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: The future was black plastic, blue cases, DVD playback and screenshots that looked almost too polished for a bedroom television.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: On PC, the future was messier and more personal: installs, patches, graphics settings, Battle.net accounts and games that could swallow whole evenings.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: For UK players, the month often depended on what had actually reached the shelf, what the magazines had already reviewed, and what an import advert claimed was possible.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
